problem_summary: 2012 MCM/ICM Problem C (ICM, "Modeling for Crime Busting"): given a company message-traffic network of ~83 nodes (employees), a known set of conspirators, non-conspirators, and a list of conspiratorial topics, produce a priority list ranking all unknown nodes by likelihood of being part of an embezzlement/credit-card-fraud conspiracy, identify the conspiracy leader, and show how semantic/text analysis of message content would improve the detection. The paper attacks this with a custom node-ranking scheme ("iRank") plus a leadership-detection step and a content-aware extension.
models_used: iRank model — a custom node-suspicion ranking algorithm built as a hybrid of PageRank and Social Network Analysis (SNA), with a two-phase structure: (1) Initialization that assigns each unknown node an initial suspicion score, (2) Iteration that propagates suspicion across weighted directed edges to a fixed point

Modified PageRank with weighted directed edges — iterative score propagation IR(n) = Σ IR(x)·α over in/out neighbors, with edge weights α_{i-j} that are NOT uniform (standard PageRank can't handle weighted links) and that are normalized so Σα=1 per node; convergence argued via the Markov property to a stationary distribution

Initialization scoring function — a 5-term averaged composite per node combining: fraction of messages sent to conspirators (ms_n/O(n)), fraction received from conspirators (mr_n/I(n)), fraction of conspiratorial messages sent (ts_n/O(n)) and received (tr_n/I(n)), plus a closeness term d(n); each term scaled by an adjusting weight w to put units on a common scale

Harmonic-series saturation terms hs(n), hr(n) — used inside max(...) against the raw send/receive fractions so that repeatedly sending/receiving conspiratorial messages to/from known conspirators contributes with diminishing returns

Closeness-based separation term d(n) = C_n · CL(n_c)/CL(n_n), using a closeness centrality C(n_i) = [Σ dist(n_i,n_j)]^{-1} (inverse total geodesic distance) to measure how much closer a node sits to the conspirator cluster than to the non-conspirator cluster — an SNA clustering idea grounded in Small-World theory

Leadership-detection model — a two-feature SNA outlier analysis plotting Neighborhood Connectivity vs. Average Shortest Path Length; leaders are identified as the abnormal off-trend nodes (high neighborhood connectivity but not minimal shortest path, consistent with a hub bridging functional sub-groups). Identified No.16 Jerome and No.10 Dolores

Semantic Network Analysis + text-analysis extension (Task 3) — replaces the constant topic-based link weight with a content-derived link-weight function f(m) ∈ [-1,1] learned by similarity to messages sent/received by known conspirators vs. non-conspirators (a supervised-learning framing), feeding a content-weighted version of the iterative ranking equation

Parameter estimation from sample statistics — the standardizing weights (w_ms, w_mr, w_ts, w_tr) were fit from observed send/receive count tables (e.g., Task 1 w = {1.2, 1.2, 1.1, 1.25}; re-estimated for Task 2 as {1.3, 1.2, 1.13, 1.27}) assuming sample distribution ≈ population distribution

Conspirator-group-size estimation for thresholding — used the observation that the 7 known conspirators already account for ~40% of conspiratorial messages to argue total conspirators < 20, then set a manual critical IR cutoff on the priority list
key_techniques: Domain-driven feature design: every term in the scoring function maps to a stated criminology assumption (conspirators are more active, non-conspirators rarely initiate conspiratorial topics, initiators are more suspicious than responders), so the math is motivated rather than arbitrary

Disambiguation via the model itself: exploited duplicate names (two 'Elsie's No.7/No.37; two 'Jerome's No.16/No.34) as a built-in validation — the model correctly picks the high-connection node, demonstrating discriminative power
Edge-weighting with an indicator function I_{n-x} that boosts α for links carrying a conspiratorial topic, so suspicious messages propagate more rank than irrelevant ones
Harmonic-series saturation to prevent a single high-volume contact from dominating the score (diminishing-returns shaping)
Closeness centrality ratio (to-conspirator vs. to-non-conspirator) as a cluster-separation signal, justified empirically with a scatter plot of abnormal nodes
Leader detection reframed as 2-D outlier spotting on graph-theoretic features (neighborhood connectivity vs. avg shortest path) rather than a separate model — cheap and visual
Re-estimating parameters cleanly when Task 2 adds a new conspiratorial topic and a known conspirator (Elsie), showing the model generalizes by re-fitting rather than re-architecting
Iterating the rank equation 1000 times to convergence and reporting the stabilized priority list
Supervised-learning framing for the text extension: anchoring link weights at +1 (conspirator messages) and -1 (non-conspirator messages) and scoring new messages by similarity
Validation by visualization: rendering the criminal network graph and the closeness/centrality scatter plots so a judge can see the separation, not just trust the numbers
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on three fronts. First, it builds a NAMED, original method (iRank) rather than applying a textbook algorithm verbatim — and it justifies the originality precisely: it states why plain PageRank fails here (can't handle weighted/typed links and seeds randomly) and why plain SNA fails (ignores node identities/labels), then fuses the two to cover both gaps. That is exactly the 'modeling judgment' judges reward. Second, it delivers concrete, actionable answers: a ranked priority list with numeric IR scores, named the likely leaders (Jerome No.16, Dolores No.10), and named specific well-hidden conspirators (Inez, Bob) that content analysis would catch — the problem asked who, and it answered who. Third, it handles all four tasks coherently with one consistent framework that scales: Task 1 detection, Task 2 re-fit with new info, Task 3 semantic/text upgrade, Task 4 generalization to other networks (biological infection spread). It pairs this with an honest, specific weakness section (ignores time/sequence; threshold must be set manually) and a literature base of relevant, on-topic citations (Xu & Chen, Morselli, Natarajan), signaling the team knew the real field of criminal-network analysis. The combination of a defensible novel model, definitive named outputs, end-to-end task coverage, and credible self-critique is the standard Outstanding profile.
section_highlights: The summary is strong and front-loaded: it names the method (iRank), states the gap it fills versus PageRank and SNA, and previews the deliverables (quantitative suspicion priority list, leader detection, semantic-analysis improvement catching Inez/Bob). The structure follows the four tasks one-to-one and keeps a single model thread through all of them, so the reader never loses the spine. A dedicated 'Dataset Observations and Basic Statistical Analysis' section up front earns the assumptions — every modeling assumption (conspirators more active; non-conspirators receive but rarely initiate conspiratorial topics) is backed by a bar-chart statistic before it is used, which is excellent practice. A clean Symbols-and-Definitions table makes the dense notation navigable. Each task ends with an explicit Strength/Weakness 'Evaluation and Discussion' block, and the closing Task 4 broadens the model to other domains (disease spread in biological networks) — showing generality, a classic ICM closer. The figures do real work: the closeness scatter and the connectivity-vs-shortest-path scatter visually isolate the abnormal leader nodes, and the network-graph visualization validates the final ranking.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Key parameters are weakly justified: the adjusting weights w_ms/w_mr/w_ts/w_tr (≈1.1–1.27) are asserted 'from sample statistics' but the derivation is never shown, and the '5' and '2' denominators (equal-importance averaging) are assumed without sensitivity analysis

Notation/formula inconsistencies: the iterative equation is written as IR(n) in Task 1 but PR(n) in Task 3; the harmonic-series terms hs/hr and the closeness scaling are described informally and would be hard to reproduce exactly from the text

The leadership result rests on visually spotting two outliers in a scatter plot with no quantitative outlier criterion or confidence — it identifies Jerome and Dolores but doesn't establish a decision rule

No principled threshold: the model admits it cannot set the conspirator/non-conspirator cutoff before seeing the ranked list; the 'total conspirators < 20' bound comes from a single heuristic (7 knowns ≈ 40% of conspiratorial messages) rather than an estimator
Time and message sequence are explicitly discarded (stated assumption), which the paper itself flags as losing the initiator-vs-responder signal that is central to assigning guilt

The entire Task 3 semantic/text-analysis extension is unimplemented — the message contents were not provided, so f(m) is only described as a procedure and demonstrated narratively (Inez 'tired'/'exhausted', Spanish-word codes) rather than run on data

Some embedded chart tables in the markdown appear to be synthetic/placeholder (e.g., the perfectly linear closeness scatter 0.1→2.0, the 1..96 ramp in Figure 4), so the figures as transcribed don't fully corroborate the claimed separation
Reproducibility gap: no pseudocode or explicit complexity bound is given despite the claim of being efficient versus high-dimensional matrix operations
reusable_patterns: The 'modify a famous algorithm and name it' pattern: take a well-known method (PageRank), state precisely what it can't do for your problem, splice in a second paradigm (SNA closeness/clustering) to cover the gap, and brand the result — gives judges a clear novelty hook

Earn every assumption with an upfront statistics section: compute simple in/out-degree and topic-frequency comparisons between known classes, then cite those numbers as the justification for each assumption and feature

Two-phase node scoring: a feature-engineered initialization (composite of normalized, domain-meaningful ratios) followed by graph propagation to a fixed point — a clean template for any 'rank the nodes by hidden property' network problem
Use label leakage as built-in validation: when the data has duplicate/ambiguous entities, show your model resolves them correctly as evidence of discriminative power
Indicator-weighted edges: multiply edge weights by an indicator for the 'important' edge type so the signal of interest propagates faster than noise in any diffusion/ranking model
Detect special roles (leaders/hubs) as outliers in a 2-D plot of two graph-centrality features rather than building a separate model — cheap, visual, and judge-friendly
Saturating contributions with a harmonic series (or any concave transform) to stop high-volume single relationships from dominating a score
Re-fit, don't re-build: when the prompt adds new information in a later task, re-estimate the same model's parameters and show the updated ranking — demonstrates robustness and saves page space
Close with cross-domain generalization: re-cast the model for an unrelated network (here, infection spread in biological cells via 1/distance weights) to show the framework is not problem-specific
Always pair each model section with an explicit Strength/Weakness block naming concrete, specific limitations (time ignored, threshold manual) — credible self-critique reads as maturity to judges
